lessons.title: What Light Is
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Flip a switch and a room fills with light instantly. But what *is* light? It is the same kind of thing as a radio signal or an X-ray, just a different flavor. Understanding it explains rainbows, cameras, and why you can see at all.

**Build.** Light is an **electromagnetic wave** — a ripple of electric and magnetic energy that travels through empty space, needing no air or water to carry it. Like a wave on a pond, it has a **wavelength** (the distance from one crest to the next). Light's wavelength is tiny, far smaller than a hair.

When we study how light travels and bounces, we often draw it as a straight arrow called a **ray**. This is the **ray model**: light travels in **straight lines** until it hits something. That is why a flashlight makes a straight beam and why shadows have sharp edges.

Light is also astonishingly fast: about **300,000 kilometers per second** in a vacuum — around the Earth seven times in one second. We write its speed as $c$ (just a label for that number).

All electromagnetic waves form the **electromagnetic (EM) spectrum**, ordered by wavelength: radio (longest), then microwave, infrared, then **visible light** (the only part our eyes detect), then ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays (shortest). Visible light is a tiny slice of the whole spectrum.

**Recap.** Light is a fast electromagnetic wave that travels in straight rays at about 300,000 km/s; the colors we see are just a narrow band of a much larger spectrum.

items.prompt: In physics, light is best described as what kind of thing?
choices: A stream of tiny hot particles like dust
An electromagnetic wave
A flow of air pushed by a source
A type of sound that is too high to hear
items.explanation: Light is an electromagnetic wave — a ripple of electric and magnetic energy. It is not air or sound (those need a material to travel through, and sound is a pressure wave in matter). Light can cross the empty vacuum of space, which is how sunlight reaches us.

items.prompt: Roughly how fast does light travel in a vacuum?
choices: About 300 kilometers per second
About 300,000 kilometers per second
About 1,000 kilometers per hour
It is instant — it has no speed
items.explanation: Light moves at about 300,000 km per second, fast enough to circle Earth roughly seven times in one second. It is incredibly fast but not infinite or instant — sunlight, for example, takes about eight minutes to reach us.

items.prompt: Why does a flashlight beam appear as a straight line and shadows have sharp edges?
choices: Light curves gently around objects
Light travels in straight lines until it hits something
Light is pulled downward by gravity
Light bounces randomly in all directions from the source
items.explanation: In the ray model, light travels in straight lines. An object blocks the straight rays behind it, leaving a sharp shadow. If light curved around objects easily, shadows would be blurry and beams would bend.

items.prompt: What is the 'ray model' of light useful for?
choices: Drawing light as straight arrows to track where it goes
Measuring the temperature of a light source
Proving light is made of sound
Explaining why light needs air to travel
items.explanation: The ray model represents light as straight arrows (rays), which makes it easy to trace how light reflects and bends. It is a simplified tool for geometry; it does not measure temperature, and light does not need air to travel.

items.prompt: Visible light is what portion of the full electromagnetic spectrum?
choices: The entire spectrum — there is nothing else
A tiny slice of a much larger spectrum
Exactly half of the spectrum
The longest-wavelength part of the spectrum
items.explanation: Visible light is only a small band of the EM spectrum, which also includes radio, microwave, infrared, ultraviolet, X-rays, and gamma rays. Our eyes happen to detect just that narrow slice; the rest is invisible to us.

items.prompt: Which list places electromagnetic waves in order from longest wavelength to shortest?
choices: X-rays, visible light, radio
Radio, visible light, X-rays
Visible light, radio, X-rays
Gamma rays, radio, microwave
items.explanation: Going from longest to shortest wavelength: radio, microwave, infrared, visible, ultraviolet, X-rays, gamma. So radio (long) → visible (middle) → X-rays (short) is correct. The reversed order puts the shortest first, which is wrong.

items.prompt: What does 'wavelength' mean for a light wave?
choices: How bright the light is
The distance from one wave crest to the next
How long the light has existed
The total length of the beam
items.explanation: Wavelength is the distance between repeating points of the wave, like crest to crest. It is not brightness (that is the amount of light energy) nor the length of the whole beam. Different wavelengths of visible light appear as different colors.

items.prompt: How can sunlight reach Earth across the empty vacuum of space?
choices: Air in space carries it to us
An electromagnetic wave needs no material to travel through
The Sun blows it toward us like wind
Gravity drags the light across the gap
items.explanation: Electromagnetic waves can travel through a vacuum, needing no air or other medium. That is a key difference from sound, which must travel through matter. There is essentially no air in space, yet light crosses it just fine.

items.prompt: Which everyday device uses a part of the EM spectrum that our eyes cannot see?
choices: A candle, which gives off visible light
A TV remote, which sends infrared signals
A green laser pointer
A red traffic light
items.explanation: A TV remote uses infrared, which is invisible to human eyes but still electromagnetic. The candle, green laser, and red light are all visible light. This shows the spectrum extends beyond what we can see.

lessons.title: Reflection & Mirrors
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Look in a mirror and a copy of you looks back. Catch sunlight on a watch and you can flick a bright dot across a wall. Both are **reflection** — light bouncing off a surface — and it follows one neat rule.

**Build.** When a ray of light hits a surface, it bounces off. To describe the angles, we first draw a **normal**: an imaginary line that is perpendicular (at 90 degrees) to the surface at the point of contact. The incoming ray is the **incident ray**, and the bounced ray is the **reflected ray**.

The **law of reflection** says: the **angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection** (both measured from the normal, not the surface). Think of a ball thrown at a smooth floor — it bounces away at the same slant it came in.

A flat (**plane**) mirror has a smooth surface, so all rays obey the rule together and you get a clear image. A rough surface scatters rays in all directions (**diffuse reflection**) — that is why paper looks matte, not mirror-like.

**Curved** mirrors bend the rule to useful effect. A **concave** mirror (caved inward, like the inside of a spoon) can gather rays to a point and magnify — used in makeup mirrors and telescopes. A **convex** mirror (bulging outward) spreads rays and shows a wide view — used in car side mirrors and shop security mirrors.

Crucially, you see an object because light from a source bounces off it and travels into your eyes. Your eyes do **not** emit light.

**Recap.** Light reflects so that the angle in equals the angle out, measured from the normal; smooth surfaces give clear images, and we see by catching reflected light.

items.prompt: The law of reflection states that:
choices: The angle of incidence equals the angle of reflection
Light always bounces straight back the way it came
The reflected ray is twice the angle of the incident ray
Light slows down when it reflects
items.explanation: Angle in equals angle out, both measured from the normal. Light only bounces straight back if it hits exactly along the normal; at a slant it leaves at the same slant on the other side. Reflection changes direction, not speed.

items.prompt: Why are you able to see a wooden table that is not itself glowing?
choices: Your eyes send out rays that feel the table
Light from a source reflects off the table into your eyes
The table emits its own light
Air around the table carries its image to you
items.explanation: We see non-glowing objects because light from a source (Sun, lamp) bounces off them and enters our eyes. Eyes detect light; they do not emit it. This is a very common misconception worth correcting: vision is receiving, not sending.

items.prompt: The angles in the law of reflection are measured from what reference line?
choices: The surface itself
The normal (the line perpendicular to the surface)
The edge of the mirror
The horizon
items.explanation: Angles are measured from the normal, the line at 90 degrees to the surface where the ray hits. Measuring from the surface instead would give different numbers, so always use the normal as the reference.

items.prompt: A ray strikes a flat mirror at 30 degrees from the normal. At what angle does it leave?
choices: 60 degrees from the normal
30 degrees from the normal
0 degrees (straight back)
90 degrees from the normal
items.explanation: By the law of reflection the outgoing angle equals the incoming angle: 30 degrees from the normal. It does not double to 60, and it only goes straight back if the incoming angle were 0.

items.prompt: Why does a sheet of paper look matte (not mirror-like), even though it reflects light?
choices: Paper absorbs all the light that hits it
Its rough surface scatters rays in many directions (diffuse reflection)
Paper does not obey the law of reflection
Paper only reflects invisible light
items.explanation: Each tiny patch of paper still obeys the law of reflection, but the surface is rough, so rays scatter every which way — called diffuse reflection. That scattering prevents a clear image. A smooth surface keeps the rays organized, giving a mirror image.

items.prompt: Which mirror is curved outward (bulging) and gives a wide, shrunken view, like a car's side mirror?
choices: Concave mirror
Convex mirror
Plane mirror
Two-way mirror
items.explanation: A convex mirror bulges outward and spreads rays apart, giving a wide field of view with smaller images — ideal for car and shop mirrors. A concave mirror caves inward and can magnify; a plane mirror is flat.

items.prompt: A makeup mirror that magnifies your face is which type?
choices: Convex (bulging outward)
Concave (caved inward, like a spoon's inside)
Perfectly flat (plane)
A rough, diffuse surface
items.explanation: A concave mirror caves inward and can gather rays to enlarge a nearby image, which is why makeup and shaving mirrors magnify. A convex mirror would shrink the view, and a flat mirror keeps the same size.

items.prompt: In a dark room with no light source at all, why can't you see objects in a mirror?
choices: Mirrors stop working in the dark
There is no light to reflect off the objects into your eyes
Your eyes need to warm up first
The mirror absorbs the darkness
items.explanation: Seeing requires light bouncing from objects into your eyes. With no source, there is no light to reflect, so nothing is visible. The mirror is fine; it simply has no light to redirect. This reinforces that vision depends on received light.

items.prompt: If a ray hits a mirror exactly along the normal (head-on, 0 degrees), where does it go?
choices: It reflects straight back along the same path
It slides sideways along the surface
It leaves at 90 degrees
It passes through the mirror
items.explanation: With an incidence angle of 0, the reflection angle is also 0, so the ray returns straight back along its incoming path. It cannot leave at 90 degrees (that would be along the surface), and a mirror reflects rather than transmits.

lessons.title: Refraction & Lenses
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Put a straw in a glass of water and it looks bent or broken at the surface. The straw is fine — the light is what bends. This bending, called **refraction**, is the secret behind eyeglasses, cameras, magnifiers, and rainbows.

**Build.** Light travels fastest in a vacuum (and nearly as fast in air) but **slows down** in materials like water or glass. When a ray crosses the boundary between two materials *at an angle*, the change in speed makes it **change direction** — that is refraction. Going into a slower material (air into glass), the ray bends **toward** the normal (the perpendicular line). Coming back out into a faster material, it bends away. Hit the surface straight-on and it slows but does not turn.

A **prism** (a triangular glass block) bends light, and because each color slows by a slightly different amount, white light fans out into a band of colors — red bends least, violet most. This splitting is called **dispersion**, and it is how rainbows form.

A **lens** is a shaped piece of glass or plastic that uses refraction on purpose. A **converging (convex)** lens is thicker in the middle and bends parallel rays inward to meet at a **focal point** — used in magnifiers, cameras, and the human eye. A **diverging (concave)** lens is thinner in the middle and spreads rays apart — used to correct nearsightedness.

**Recap.** Light slows in denser materials, and crossing a boundary at an angle bends it; lenses harness this bending to focus or spread light for glasses, cameras, and magnifiers.

items.prompt: What is refraction?
choices: Light bouncing off a shiny surface
Light changing direction as it passes from one material into another
Light being absorbed and turned into heat
Light splitting into a beam and a shadow
items.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light when it crosses from one material to another and changes speed. Bouncing off a surface is reflection, and being absorbed is something different. Refraction is why a submerged straw looks bent.

items.prompt: What actually causes the light to bend at a boundary?
choices: The light speeds up in the denser material
The light changes speed (slows in glass or water)
Gravity from the glass pulls on the light
The color of the material repels the light
items.explanation: Light slows down in denser materials like glass or water, and that change in speed at an angled boundary is what bends the ray. It does not speed up going into glass, and gravity is not the cause here.

items.prompt: When light passes from air into glass at an angle, it bends:
choices: Away from the normal
Toward the normal
Exactly 90 degrees every time
It never bends entering glass
items.explanation: Entering a slower medium (air to glass), the ray bends toward the normal. Going from glass back into air, it bends away from the normal. The amount depends on the angle, not a fixed 90 degrees.

items.prompt: Why does a straw in a glass of water appear bent at the water's surface?
choices: The water physically bends the straw
Light from the underwater part refracts as it leaves the water, so it reaches your eye at a different angle
The straw reflects light back on itself
Water magnifies the straw evenly
items.explanation: Light from the submerged part bends as it exits the water into air, so your eye traces it back to the wrong spot, making the straw look bent or shifted. The straw itself is perfectly straight; refraction fools your eye.

items.prompt: A prism splits white light into a band of colors because:
choices: The prism adds color to the white light
Each color slows and bends by a slightly different amount (dispersion)
White light is actually just one color
The glass paints the light with a rainbow
items.explanation: White light is already a mix of all colors. In glass each color bends by a slightly different amount (dispersion), so they fan apart — violet bends most, red least. The prism reveals the colors; it does not add them.

items.prompt: A converging (convex) lens does what to parallel rays of light?
choices: Spreads them apart
Brings them together to meet at a focal point
Reflects them backward
Leaves them unchanged
items.explanation: A convex lens (thicker in the middle) refracts parallel rays inward so they meet at the focal point. A diverging lens would spread rays apart instead. This focusing ability is what lets cameras and eyes form sharp images.

items.prompt: Which lens is thinner in the middle and spreads light rays apart?
choices: Converging (convex) lens
Diverging (concave) lens
Plane lens
Prism lens
items.explanation: A diverging (concave) lens is thinner in the middle and spreads rays outward; it is used to correct nearsightedness. A converging (convex) lens is thicker in the middle and focuses rays together.

items.prompt: If a ray of light hits a glass surface straight-on (along the normal), what happens?
choices: It bends sharply toward the normal
It slows down but does not change direction
It reflects straight back without entering
It splits into all the colors
items.explanation: Refraction only changes direction when light crosses a boundary at an angle. Hitting straight-on (along the normal), the light slows in the glass but keeps going straight. Bending requires an angled approach.

items.prompt: A simple magnifying glass makes small print look bigger because it is what kind of lens?
choices: A diverging (concave) lens that spreads rays
A converging (convex) lens that bends rays to enlarge the image
A flat sheet of glass
A mirror that reflects the print
items.explanation: A magnifier is a converging (convex) lens; it refracts light so the object appears larger. A diverging lens would make things look smaller, and flat glass would not magnify at all.

lessons.title: Color & Vision
lessons.content:
**Hook.** Why is grass green and a banana yellow? And why do paints mix differently from stage lights? It all comes down to which wavelengths of light reach your eye.

**Build.** Color is how our eyes interpret the **wavelength** of visible light. Longer wavelengths look red; shorter ones look violet, with orange, yellow, green, and blue in between. White light (like sunlight) is all these wavelengths mixed together.

Objects have color because of what they do to light. A red apple looks red because it **reflects** red light and **absorbs** the rest. A green leaf reflects green and absorbs the others. A black object absorbs nearly all colors; a white object reflects nearly all. So the misconception that 'a red apple is full of red color' is backwards — it is rejecting red while soaking up everything else.

Mixing colors comes in two opposite kinds. **Additive mixing** combines *light*: starting from darkness, red, green, and blue (RGB) light add up to white. This is how TV and phone screens work. **Subtractive mixing** combines *pigments* (paint or ink): each pigment absorbs (subtracts) some wavelengths, so cyan, magenta, and yellow stacked together absorb everything and look black. Paints get darker as you mix; lights get brighter.

Your **eye** works like a camera: a convex lens focuses light onto the **retina** (a light-sensitive layer at the back), which sends signals to the brain. A camera does the same with a lens and a sensor.

**Recap.** Color is the wavelength your eye detects; objects show the colors they reflect while absorbing the rest, light mixes additively (toward white) and pigment subtractively (toward black), and the eye focuses images much like a camera.

items.prompt: What physical property of light does our sense of color correspond to?
choices: The brightness of the light
The wavelength of the light
The speed of the light
The temperature of the room
items.explanation: Color corresponds to wavelength: long wavelengths look red, short ones look violet. Brightness is the amount of light, and the speed of light is essentially the same for all colors in a vacuum. Wavelength is the key to color.

items.prompt: Why does a red apple look red in white light?
choices: It is filled with red light inside
It reflects red light and absorbs the other colors
It absorbs red and reflects everything else
It creates red light on its own
items.explanation: A red apple reflects red wavelengths to your eye while absorbing the rest. The common backwards idea is that it 'contains' or absorbs red — in fact it rejects (reflects) red and soaks up the others. Objects show the colors they reflect.

items.prompt: What does a black object do to most of the light that hits it?
choices: Reflects almost all of it
Absorbs almost all of it
Bends almost all of it
Splits it into colors
items.explanation: Black objects absorb nearly all visible wavelengths, reflecting little back, which is why they look dark and warm up in sunlight. A white object does the opposite, reflecting nearly all colors.

items.prompt: In additive mixing, combining red, green, and blue LIGHT produces:
choices: Black
White
Brown
Gray
items.explanation: Adding red, green, and blue light together gives white — this is how screens make white from RGB pixels. Mixing toward black happens with pigments (subtractive mixing), not with light.

items.prompt: When you mix many different paints together, the result tends to get darker because:
choices: Paint adds light like a screen does
Each pigment absorbs (subtracts) more wavelengths, leaving less light reflected
Paint reflects all colors equally
Darkness is a color you are adding
items.explanation: Pigments work by subtractive mixing: each one absorbs some wavelengths, so combining many leaves fewer wavelengths to reflect, trending toward black. This is the opposite of mixing light, which trends toward white.

items.prompt: A glowing phone screen makes its colors using which method?
choices: Subtractive mixing with cyan, magenta, yellow pigments
Additive mixing with red, green, blue light
Reflecting room light only
Bending sunlight through a prism
items.explanation: Screens emit light and use additive mixing of red, green, and blue to build colors. Subtractive (CMY) mixing is for inks and paints that absorb light. A screen produces its own light rather than relying on reflection.

items.prompt: What is the role of the retina in the eye?
choices: It focuses the light, like a lens
It is the light-sensitive layer where the image forms and signals go to the brain
It controls how much light enters
It produces the light the eye sees with
items.explanation: The retina is the light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye where the focused image lands and is converted to nerve signals. Focusing is done by the lens; the eye detects light rather than producing it.

items.prompt: How is a camera similar to the human eye?
choices: Both emit light to scan the scene
Both use a lens to focus light onto a sensitive surface (sensor or retina)
Both rely on sound to form images
Neither uses a lens
items.explanation: A camera focuses light with a convex lens onto a sensor, just as the eye focuses light onto the retina. Neither emits light to see; both collect incoming light. This parallel is why cameras are a good model for vision.

items.prompt: A white shirt looks white because it:
choices: Absorbs all colors of light
Reflects nearly all colors of light back to your eye
Emits its own white light
Bends light into a rainbow
items.explanation: White surfaces reflect nearly all visible wavelengths together, which your eye reads as white. A surface that absorbed all colors would look black, and ordinary cloth does not emit its own light.
